shakyamuni.summary: The historical Buddha of early Buddhism and a reference for many Mahayana traditions; he represents awakening through wisdom and compassion. Statues are usually male, with ushnisha and monastic robes.
shakyamuni.mindfulness_hint: Images of Shakyamuni invite a pause: notice what is felt in the body, then choose a response with intention rather than autopilot habit.

mudras_common.name: Bhumisparsha mudra (earth-touching)
mudras_common.meaning: Calling the earth as witness — steadiness and clear presence before distraction; an anchor in difficulty.

mudras_common.name: Dharmachakra mudra (turning the wheel)
mudras_common.meaning: Turning the wheel of teaching — sharing understanding with an open mind.

mudras_common.name: Dhyana mudra (meditation)
mudras_common.meaning: Settling body and breath — finding stillness even on busy days.

implements.name: Alms bowl (in some statues)
implements.meaning: Simple sufficiency and receiving what life offers without extravagance.

amitabha.summary: The Buddha of infinite light and life, central to Pure Land traditions; he symbolizes faith and aspiration toward awakening. Often shown seated in meditation, similar to a Buddha form but linked to the Western Pure Land.
amitabha.mindfulness_hint: Boundless light can mean: soften the small fearful self, widen the heart toward one difficult person or moment.

mudras_common.name: Dhyana mudra
mudras_common.meaning: Inner reception and calm — making room to ‘return’ to what is wholesome.

mudras_common.name: Welcoming / guiding gesture (common in Pure Land art)
mudras_common.meaning: An open hand reaching out — compassion toward oneself and others.

implements.name: Lotus throne
implements.meaning: Purity arising from the mud — goodness growing in imperfect conditions.

avalokiteshvara.summary: The bodhisattva of compassion, often shown with a vase, willow branch, or thousand-armed form. The figure embodies listening and responding to suffering without turning away.
avalokiteshvara.mindfulness_hint: Avalokiteshvara suggests layered listening: first body (breath, tension), then sounds, then words — delaying judgment.

mudras_common.name: Varada / blessing gesture
mudras_common.meaning: Offering care and patience — not only when it is easy.

mudras_common.name: Holding vase or willow
mudras_common.meaning: Gentle cleansing of ‘dust’ — flexible mind, less rigid grasping.

implements.name: Willow branch or vase
implements.meaning: Gentle, flexible compassion in daily friction.

implements.name: Lotus or māṇi jewel
implements.meaning: Clarity and vow arising from muddy conditions.

kshitigarbha.summary: The bodhisattva who vows to save beings, especially those in difficult realms; he stands for patience and long-term responsibility. Often shown shaved head or simple crown, with khakkhara and wish-fulfilling jewel — distinct from Guanyin’s vase/lotus.
kshitigarbha.mindfulness_hint: Kṣitigarbha suggests patience: heart and habits change slowly; less self-blame when change is not instant.

mudras_common.name: Holding khakkhara (staff)
mudras_common.meaning: Long-haul resolve — a ‘walking stick’ for dark stretches of life.

mudras_common.name: Giving / bestowal (some statues)
mudras_common.meaning: Offering time, care, and listening — not only when convenient.

implements.name: Khakkhara (six-ring staff)
implements.meaning: Opening the ‘gates’ of stuck habits — supporting those caught in addiction or despair.

implements.name: Cintāmaṇi (wish-fulfilling jewel)
implements.meaning: True aspiration — doing good with clear intention, not show.

maitreya.summary: The bodhisattva of the future, often joyful and generous; in East Asia the ‘laughing Buddha’ with large belly and cloth sack is widespread. Differs from solemn, ushnisha Shakyamuni.
maitreya.mindfulness_hint: Maitreya’s smile invites a small smile and relaxed shoulders — joy can start in the body without a big reason.

mudras_common.name: Meditation or hands on knees
mudras_common.meaning: Ease that does not depend on perfect outer conditions.

mudras_common.name: Varada (giving)
mudras_common.meaning: Sharing joy and surplus with others.

implements.name: Cloth sack
implements.meaning: Carrying what is enough to share — simplicity and generosity.

manjushri.summary: The bodhisattva of wisdom, often with sword and scripture, sometimes a lion mount. He represents sharp, compassionate insight rather than mere cleverness.
manjushri.mindfulness_hint: Mañjuśrī reminds us to watch thoughts as events in the mind — arising and passing — before fully believing the ‘me / them’ story.

mudras_common.name: Teaching or holding sutra
mudras_common.meaning: Aligning speech and study with kindness.

mudras_common.name: Sword of wisdom
mudras_common.meaning: Cutting through confusion — naming delusion without cruelty.

implements.name: Prajñāpāramitā sutra
implements.meaning: Wisdom carried into life — not only book knowledge.

samantabhadra.summary: The bodhisattva of great vow and practice, often with white elephant and lotus. He embodies ethical, embodied mindfulness in motion.
samantabhadra.mindfulness_hint: Samantabhadra suggests mindful movement: one step, one kind word — not only on the cushion.

mudras_common.name: Offering or prayerful gesture
mudras_common.meaning: Dedicating action to benefit — small vows in ordinary tasks.

implements.name: White elephant
implements.meaning: Power and steadiness directed toward compassion.

implements.name: Lotus
implements.meaning: Clarity rising from ordinary ground.

bhaisajyaguru.summary: The Medicine Buddha of lapis light, often with medicine bowl and healing symbolism. Distinguished from Amitabha when medicine / healing signs are clear.
bhaisajyaguru.mindfulness_hint: Bhaiṣajyaguru points to basics: sleep, food, movement, and rest with awareness — a base for wisdom and care.

mudras_common.name: Medicine-bowl gesture
mudras_common.meaning: Holding well-being for self and others — practical care.

implements.name: Medicine bowl
implements.meaning: Healing offered without hoarding — sharing remedies and attention.

implements.name: Lapis / blue light imagery
implements.meaning: Calm, cooling presence in pain or anxiety.

vairochana.summary: The cosmic / dharma-body Buddha, central in many mandalas and esoteric halls. Mudrās such as teaching or wisdom-realm gestures appear on large main icons.
vairochana.mindfulness_hint: Vairocana suggests seeing layers: body, feeling, meaning — without rushing to one fixed story.

mudras_common.name: Dharmachakra or teaching mudrā
mudras_common.meaning: Teaching as relational — turning understanding toward others.

implements.name: Wheel or central maṇḍala role
implements.meaning: Interconnection — one center radiating clarity.

mahasthamaprapta.summary: Often paired with Amitabha and Avalokiteshvara in the Western triad; male form, lotus or vase motifs. Distinguish carefully from Guanyin when attributes are unclear.
mahasthamaprapta.mindfulness_hint: Mahāsthāmaprāpta: wisdom is not only information — seeing motive, consequence, and kindness in action.

mudras_common.name: Meditation or offering
mudras_common.meaning: Steady vow alongside compassion.

implements.name: Lotus
implements.meaning: Pure aspiration amid ordinary life.

tara.summary: A female bodhisattva, often one leg extended, holding utpala (blue lotus), in Tibetan and related traditions. Not the same as Guanyin when iconography differs.
tara.mindfulness_hint: Tārā: when afraid, hand on chest, slow breath — remembering you also deserve comfort, not only others.

mudras_common.name: Varada / refuge gesture
mudras_common.meaning: Safety and shelter offered and received.

implements.name: Utpala flower
implements.meaning: Compassion unfolding step by step.

akshobhya.summary: The ‘immovable’ Buddha of the East, often blue, with earth-touching gesture in Five-Buddha schemes.
akshobhya.mindfulness_hint: Akṣobhya: anger can be felt in the body; you need not drive the car of action with it — pause one beat.

mudras_common.name: Bhumisparsha
mudras_common.meaning: Unshakable presence — witness like earth.

implements.name: Eastern / vajra imagery (tradition-dependent)
implements.meaning: Stability as a direction of practice.

ratnasambhava.summary: Jewel-born Buddha of the South, golden, often Varada mudra, in Five-Buddha maṇḍalas.
ratnasambhava.mindfulness_hint: Ratnasambhava: note one thing that is enough today — food, sleep, one listener — easing hollow striving.

mudras_common.name: Varada (giving)
mudras_common.meaning: Generosity and abundance of heart.

implements.name: Jewel
implements.meaning: Precious qualities to share, not hoard.

amoghasiddhi.summary: The ‘unfailing success’ Buddha of the North, green, often Abhaya mudra, in Five-Buddha systems.
amoghasiddhi.mindfulness_hint: Amoghasiddhi: finish one small thing today instead of a huge list never started.

mudras_common.name: Abhaya (fearlessness)
mudras_common.meaning: Confidence without aggression.

acala.summary: A wrathful wisdom king with fire, sword, or rope — fierce compassion, not gentle seated Buddha forms.
acala.mindfulness_hint: Acala: boundaries can be kind — saying ‘no’ to what harms without hatred.

mudras_common.name: Wrathful mudrās / weapon holds
mudras_common.meaning: Cutting through denial with clarity.

implements.name: Sword
implements.meaning: Cutting delusion, not people.

implements.name: Rope / bond imagery
implements.meaning: Restraining harm skillfully.

prajnaparamita.summary: Personification of the Perfection of Wisdom — often female, with book or lotus. Distinguish from Tārā when attributes differ.
prajnaparamita.mindfulness_hint: Prajñāpāramitā: any fixed ‘I know for sure’ can soften when more data and kindness enter.

mudras_common.name: Teaching or holding text
mudras_common.meaning: Wisdom embodied — study serving compassion.

implements.name: Prajñāpāramitā text / lotus
implements.meaning: Insight that frees rather than hardens.
